nodes.name: Jazz
nodes.description: Pioneering jazz musicians and their landmark recordings

items.prompt: Which trumpeter and singer, nicknamed 'Satchmo,' recorded 'What a Wonderful World' and helped define jazz improvisation?
items.explanation:
Trumpeter, vocalist (1901–1971), from New Orleans, Louisiana
Pioneered jazz improvisation with his Hot Five and Hot Seven recordings (1925–1928), shifting jazz from ensemble music to soloist-driven art
'What a Wonderful World' (1967) became his signature song, though it initially flopped in the U.S.
Nicknamed 'Satchmo' (short for 'Satchel Mouth') and 'Pops'
First African American to host a nationally broadcast radio show (1937)
His gravelly singing voice was as influential as his trumpet playing — he popularized scat singing
Played for over five decades; his 1964 recording of 'Hello, Dolly!' knocked the Beatles off #1

items.prompt: Which bandleader and pianist held a residency at the Cotton Club and composed 'Take the A Train' and 'Mood Indigo'?
items.explanation:
Composer, bandleader, pianist (1899–1974), born in Washington, D.C.
Led his orchestra for over 50 years; composed or co-composed over 1,000 pieces
The Cotton Club residency in Harlem (1927–1931) made him nationally famous via radio broadcasts
'Take the A Train' (1941), written by Billy Strayhorn, became the orchestra's signature theme
Composed extended suites like *Black, Brown and Beige* (1943) that expanded jazz beyond the three-minute single
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from Nixon in 1969
Nominated for a Pulitzer Prize in 1965; the board overruled the committee, awarding no prize — he remarked, 'Fate is being kind to me'

items.prompt: Which trumpeter recorded *Kind of Blue* (1959), the best-selling jazz album of all time?
items.explanation:
Trumpeter, bandleader, composer (1926–1991), from Alton, Illinois
*Kind of Blue* (1959) is the best-selling jazz album of all time with 5+ million copies sold in the U.S. alone
Reinvented himself across multiple eras: cool jazz (*Birth of the Cool*, 1957), modal jazz (*Kind of Blue*), fusion (*Bitches Brew*, 1970)
His first great quintet featured John Coltrane and Red Garland; his second featured Wayne Shorter and Herbie Hancock
*Bitches Brew* (1970) fused jazz with rock and funk, launching the jazz-fusion movement
Notorious for playing with his back to the audience and his spare, lyrical trumpet tone
Inducted into the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame in 2006 for his influence on rock and funk

items.prompt: Which saxophonist recorded *A Love Supreme* in 1965 and was a member of Miles Davis's first great quintet?
items.explanation:
Tenor and soprano saxophonist (1926–1967), from Hamlet, North Carolina
*A Love Supreme* (1965) is a four-part spiritual suite; it sold over 500,000 copies, extraordinary for avant-garde jazz
Developed the 'sheets of sound' technique — rapid, densely packed arpeggios that redefined saxophone playing
Played in Miles Davis's first great quintet and Thelonious Monk's quartet before leading his own groups
*Giant Steps* (1960) introduced chord changes so complex ('Coltrane changes') they remain a test for jazz musicians
Died of liver cancer at age 40; some scholars link it to his earlier heroin addiction
Canonized as a saint by the African Orthodox Church in San Francisco

items.prompt: Which alto saxophonist, nicknamed 'Bird,' co-invented bebop jazz in the 1940s?
items.explanation:
Alto saxophonist, composer (1920–1955), from Kansas City, Kansas
Co-invented bebop with Dizzy Gillespie in the mid-1940s, revolutionizing jazz with fast tempos and complex harmonies
Nicknamed 'Bird' (short for 'Yardbird'); New York's premier jazz club Birdland was named after him
His improvisations on songs like 'Ko-Ko' (1945) and 'Ornithology' (1946) set new standards for virtuosity and creativity
Recorded with the legendary 'Savoy Sessions' and 'Dial Sessions' between 1944 and 1948
Died at age 34 from a heart attack complicated by cirrhosis, ulcers, and pneumonia; the coroner estimated his age at 53
Graffiti reading 'Bird Lives!' appeared across New York City immediately after his death

items.prompt: Which pianist and composer wrote 'Round Midnight' and was known for his angular, percussive playing style?
items.explanation:
Pianist, composer (1917–1982), from Rocky Mount, North Carolina; raised in New York City
'Round Midnight' (1944) is the most-recorded jazz composition of all time
His angular, dissonant piano style and use of silence were initially dismissed as amateurish, later recognized as visionary
Second-most-recorded jazz composer after Ellington; wrote 70+ original compositions
Was featured on the cover of *Time* magazine in 1964 — only the third jazz musician so honored (after Armstrong and Brubeck)
Lost his cabaret card (NYC performance license) for six years (1951–1957) after a narcotics arrest; could not play NYC clubs
Spent his final six years in seclusion at the home of patron Baroness Nica de Koenigswarter, barely speaking or playing

items.prompt: Which vocalist, known as the 'First Lady of Song,' recorded the definitive *Songbook* series interpreting the Great American Songbook?
items.explanation:
Vocalist (1917–1996), from Newport News, Virginia
Recorded the eight-volume *Songbook* series (1956–1964), definitive interpretations of Cole Porter, Gershwin, Ellington, and others
Won 13 Grammys — the first African American woman to win a Grammy (Best Individual Jazz Vocal, 1958)
Known for flawless intonation, three-octave range, and virtuosic scat singing
Discovered at age 17 at an amateur night at the Apollo Theater in Harlem (1934)
Norman Granz, her manager, fought segregation by refusing to let her play venues that discriminated
Received the National Medal of Arts (1987) and the Presidential Medal of Freedom (1992)

items.prompt: Which vocalist recorded 'Strange Fruit' in 1939, a song protesting the lynching of African Americans?
items.explanation:
Vocalist (1915–1959), from Philadelphia, raised in Baltimore
'Strange Fruit' (1939) protested lynching with metaphorical lyrics comparing victims to fruit hanging from trees; *Time* named it the song of the 20th century
Nicknamed 'Lady Day' by saxophonist Lester Young, whom she nicknamed 'Prez' in return
Her emotional, behind-the-beat phrasing influenced virtually every jazz and pop vocalist who followed
Persecuted by federal narcotics agents partly for refusing to stop performing 'Strange Fruit'
Died at age 44 in a New York hospital; police arrested her on her deathbed for heroin possession
Her autobiography *Lady Sings the Blues* (1956) was adapted into a 1972 Diana Ross film

items.prompt: Which trumpeter co-invented bebop with Charlie Parker and was known for his bent trumpet and puffed cheeks?
items.explanation:
Trumpeter, bandleader, composer, singer (1917–1993), from Cheraw, South Carolina
Co-invented bebop with Charlie Parker; their 1945 recordings ('Salt Peanuts,' 'Groovin' High') defined the genre
Trademark bent trumpet (bell angled 45° upward) originated when someone accidentally sat on it; he liked the sound
Pioneered Afro-Cuban jazz by hiring Cuban percussionist Chano Pozo for his big band in 1947
Ran for President of the United States in 1964 as a write-in candidate, promising to rename the White House the 'Blues House'
His puffed-out cheeks (from a condition called laryngocele) became visually iconic
Received the National Medal of Arts and a Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award

items.prompt: Which pianist led the quartet that recorded the album *Time Out* (1959), featuring the hit 'Take Five' in 5/4 time?
items.explanation:
Pianist, composer (1920–2012), from Concord, California
*Time Out* (1959) was the first jazz album to sell one million copies; its single 'Take Five' was the first jazz single to do so
'Take Five' was actually composed by alto saxophonist Paul Desmond, not Brubeck; it's written in 5/4 time
Pioneered the use of unusual time signatures in jazz (5/4, 9/8, 7/4) — *Time Out* was built around this concept
Featured on the cover of *Time* magazine in 1954, only the second jazz musician after Louis Armstrong
Refused to perform at segregated venues, canceling 23 of 25 dates on one southern tour
Died one day before his 92nd birthday; *Time Out* was inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame (2009) and the National Recording Registry (2005)

items.prompt: Which bassist and composer recorded *Mingus Ah Um* (1959) and wrote the protest piece 'Fables of Faubus'?
items.explanation:
Bassist, composer, bandleader (1922–1979), from Nogales, Arizona; raised in Watts, Los Angeles
*Mingus Ah Um* (1959) is his most acclaimed album; it was added to the National Recording Registry in 2003
'Fables of Faubus' (1959) directly protested Arkansas governor Orval Faubus's resistance to school integration
Known for volcanic temper: once punched trombonist Jimmy Knepper in the mouth, breaking a tooth
Composed over 300 works blending jazz, gospel, classical, and Mexican folk music
'Goodbye Pork Pie Hat' (1959) was a tribute to saxophonist Lester Young, named for his trademark hat
Died of ALS at age 56; his ashes were scattered in the Ganges River in India per his wishes

items.prompt: Which pianist recorded *Head Hunters* (1973), the first jazz album to be certified platinum, and later won Album of the Year for *River: The Joni Letters*?
items.explanation:
Pianist, keyboardist, composer (b. 1940), from Chicago, Illinois
*Head Hunters* (1973) fused jazz with funk; it became the first jazz album to sell one million copies (later surpassed by *Kind of Blue*)
Member of Miles Davis's second great quintet (1963–1968), pioneering post-bop and modal jazz
'Rockit' (1983) was a groundbreaking early hip-hop/electro track, winning a Grammy for Best R&B Instrumental
Won Album of the Year at the 2008 Grammys for *River: The Joni Letters* — only the second jazz album to win (after Stan Getz's *Getz/Gilberto* in 1965)
Performed as a piano prodigy with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra at age 11
Devout practitioner of Nichiren Buddhism since 1972; credits it as a major creative influence
